metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a decision-rule specification for the 'fail closed on unreadable config' LLM gate path: name the exception type (or verdict shape) emitted, identify which config file paths and failure modes (IOError, TOML parse error, schema mismatch) count as 'unreadable', and confirm whether the gate blocks the LLM call entirely or returns a structured FAILED verdict.
findings.finding: The plan's Success Criterion 'The LLM gates fail closed on unreadable config' references a decision rule/protocol for the gate's unreadable-config path but never specifies it: which exception type is raised (or suppressed), whether the gate emits a structured FAILED verdict vs. propagates an error to the caller, and which config paths trigger 'unreadable' (pyproject, rebar.toml, XDG, all?). The existing `_read_llm_file_table` in config.py already silently degrades to env-only on `ConfigError`; the plan proposes inverting this behavior but gives the executor zero specification of the new decision rule.
findings.location: Success Criteria bullet 3: 'The LLM gates fail closed on unreadable config'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sub-bullet under Success Criteria bullet 3: 'Fail-closed means: if `_read_llm_file_table` raises or returns an unusable table AND no env-fallback yields a valid model/key, the gate raises `LLMConfigError` (not a silent degradation) so the caller receives a typed, actionable error rather than a structureless exception or an empty result.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add AC and scope to child 02cd (or a new child) explicitly covering: config parse failure / unreadable config causes the LLM gate to fail closed (raise, not silently default), with a test asserting the fail-closed path.
findings.finding: Parent success criterion 'LLM gates fail closed on unreadable config' is not covered by any child. Child 02cd-6c10-4ecf-49d6 covers only the timeout-wiring half; the 'fail closed on unreadable config' behavior is absent from its ACs, What, and Scope sections. No sibling addresses it.
findings.location: child 02cd-6c10-4ecf-49d6
findings.suggested_fix: Extend child 02cd's ACs with: 'If the config module raises on load (e.g. unreadable file, parse error), the runner raises rather than proceeding with a zero or undefined timeout; asserted by a test injecting a bad config path.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit AC line (or extend AC bullet 2) to verify that LLM gates fail closed when config is unreadable, matching the success criterion's two-part requirement.
findings.finding: Success Criteria bullet 3 names two distinct LLM-gate behaviors — 'fail closed on unreadable config' AND 'honor a wired timeout no lower than the SDK default' — but AC bullet 2 only captures 'LLM timeout wiring'. The 'fail closed on unreadable config' behavior has no covering acceptance criterion, leaving it unverifiable at close.
findings.location: Success Criteria bullet 3 vs Acceptance Criteria bullet 2
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC bullet 2 to read: '…LLM timeout wiring and fail-closed config guard…' or add a standalone line: '- [ ] LLM gate returns a safe-default refusal (not an exception propagation) when its config file is unreadable or malformed.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit AC item (and name the required regression test scenario) for 'LLM gate returns a safe/closed response when config is unreadable', separate from the timeout wiring AC.
findings.finding: The LLM gate Success Criterion has two sub-goals ('fail closed on unreadable config' AND 'honor a wired timeout no lower than the SDK default'), but the Acceptance Criteria only specify 'LLM timeout wiring landed with regression tests' — leaving the 'fail-closed on unreadable config' behavior without its own end-state-observable proof (a specific test artifact or observable outcome is not called out).
findings.location: Success Criteria bullet 3 / Acceptance Criteria bullet 2 ('LLM timeout wiring')
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC bullet 2: '… including a test asserting the gate returns a fail-closed response when config is unreadable or malformed (not only when the timeout fires).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out plan (or expand-contract migration sequence) for the LLM gate fail-closed behavioral change, covering both transient-config-unreadability scenarios and callers that handled the prior open-failure behavior.
findings.finding: The LLM gate 'fail closed on unreadable config' child inverts an existing failure-mode behavior (previously the gate presumably failed open or fell back to defaults), but the plan provides no rollback path, back-out plan, or expand-contract sequence for callers that depend on the current open/fallback behavior.
findings.location: Success Criteria: 'The LLM gates fail closed on unreadable config and honor a wired timeout no lower than the SDK default'

findings.finding: Sibling tickets touch the same file(s) with no ordering edge.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a depends_on/blocks edge to serialize, or partition the file ownership.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Coordinate docs/api-stability.md edits between 2440 and d13e: either collapse into one child, or specify which child lands first and what the other child's scope is relative to the post-merge state.
findings.finding: docs/api-stability.md is modified by two children (2440 and d13e) with no stated coordination, creating a scope overlap and merge-conflict risk: 2440 removes the hardcoded version literal while d13e updates the supported-versions wording in the same file.
findings.location: child 2440-01e4-9cc3-480c and child d13e-e1c3-8bb8-40c4
findings.suggested_fix: Assign docs/api-stability.md ownership to 2440 (remove hardcoded version, make it pointer-based or versionless). d13e's scope for that file should be limited to confirming the pointer/wording is consistent with the 3.13 addition, not rewriting version literals.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the landing order for edits to docs/concurrency.md: 3e8b (citation fixes) should land first; 3003's concurrency.md AC should explicitly note it adds to the post-3e8b state and must satisfy 3e8b's citation-exists invariant.
findings.finding: docs/concurrency.md is modified by two children — 3e8b-033c-6cd4-4711 (fix stale citations) and 3003-774d-23dc-4697 (add surfacing behavior docs) — with no stated ordering or coordination between them.
findings.location: child 3e8b-033c-6cd4-4711 and child 3003-774d-23dc-4697
findings.suggested_fix: Add to 3003's Scope: 'docs/concurrency.md edits build on the post-3e8b state; any new test citation in the surfacing-behavior section must point to an existing file.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that pydantic-ai's Agent.run_sync() (or the underlying provider/httpx layer) accepts and honors a caller-supplied wall-clock timeout, identify which SDK layer's default is the floor, and cite the API surface in the child story's AC so the 'wired timeout' claim is grounded and testable.
findings.finding: The plan's Success Criteria assert the LLM gates should 'honor a wired timeout no lower than the SDK default,' but neither the plan nor the codebase (runner.py) shows any wall-clock timeout parameter being passed to pydantic-ai's run_sync(). The SDK layer whose 'default' is the floor is unspecified (pydantic-ai? anthropic SDK? httpx?), and it is unverified that pydantic-ai's Agent.run_sync() accepts or honors a caller-supplied wall-clock timeout at all, as opposed to delegating opaquely to the provider's HTTP client defaults.
findings.location: Success Criteria bullet 3: 'honor a wired timeout no lower than the SDK default'
findings.suggested_fix: In the child story for the LLM timeout child: (1) check pydantic-ai's API for a timeout= or settings= parameter on run_sync/Agent; (2) if absent, identify the httpx/anthropic client layer that accepts it and wire cfg.timeout_s there; (3) rewrite the AC to name the concrete wiring point (e.g. 'anthropic.Anthropic(timeout=cfg.timeout_s)') and specify the minimum value relative to the provider SDK's documented default.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the specific pydantic-ai API surface (e.g. `ModelSettings(timeout=...)`, `Agent(..., settings=RunSettings(timeout=...))`, or an `httpx.Client` constructor argument) that will carry `cfg.timeout_s` into the SDK call, and confirm it exists in the pinned pydantic-ai version in `pyproject.toml`.
findings.finding: The 'LLM timeout wiring' success criterion asserts the gate will 'honor a wired timeout no lower than the SDK default,' but the existing runner code shows `LLMConfig.timeout_s` (600 s) is never passed to `agent.run_sync()` or any `model_settings`/`RunSettings` key in `runner.py`. The plan does not name the pydantic-ai API surface (e.g. `ModelSettings.timeout`, an `httpx` client constructor, or a `RunSettings` argument) that will be used to wire the timeout — this is a new capability assertion on a library surface that has not been used before in this codebase.
findings.location: Success Criteria bullet 3 ('The LLM gates fail closed on unreadable config and honor a wired timeout no lower than the SDK default'); AC bullet 2 ('LLM timeout wiring')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Establish a README.md landing order (e.g., 09d8 → 3775 → d13e) and confirm 09d8's no-reflow AC is evaluated against the fully merged README, not just its own patch.
findings.finding: README.md is modified by three independent children (09d8 adds badge row, 3775 adds docs-index link and possibly rewraps content, d13e updates supported-version statements) with no stated coordination or ordering, creating a latent reflow/conflict risk.
findings.location: child 09d8-9fd6-75f1-4de7, child 3775-8185-476f-493a, child d13e-e1c3-8bb8-40c4

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit dependency or ordering note to c3f8: CodeQL first-run triage should occur after all code-modifying siblings (02cd, 0e59, e699, d2fa, 5886) have landed, or the triage AC must be re-confirmed at epic close.
findings.finding: Child c3f8-451f-62a7-4015 (CodeQL) has an AC requiring first-run true-positive findings to be triaged before ticket close, but CodeQL's first-run results depend on the final merged codebase state — which includes code changes from multiple sibling children (02cd, 0e59, e699, d2fa, 5886). If c3f8 is implemented before siblings land, re-triage is required but not stated.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to c3f8 Scope or AC: 'First-run triage is performed against the fully merged codebase (after all reliability and maintainability siblings have landed); a re-triage confirmation is required at epic close if siblings land after the initial triage.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace 'mypy strictness ratchet started' with a stated default: name the specific flag(s) to enable (e.g. `--disallow-untyped-defs`), the target module(s) or package scope, and a maximum allowed error count (e.g. zero) enforced in CI — so the end-state is observable and unambiguous.
findings.finding: 'mypy strictness ratchet started' defers the real design decision — which flags, which modules, what error-count baseline — to the executor, and 'started' is effort-framed rather than an observable end-state.
findings.location: Success Criteria bullet: 'Typing and config-surface ratchets are in place (mypy strictness ratchet started…)' and AC bullet: 'Maintainability children (mypy ratchet start, env-var registry + drift gate) landed'
findings.suggested_fix: State: 'mypy runs with --disallow-untyped-defs on <package_name>/ with zero errors in CI (enforced via a failing step if the count regresses)'. This is a durable, in-session-verifiable end-state.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the SDK(s) in scope and their default timeout value(s) (e.g. 'OpenAI Python SDK default of 600 s') so the executor can verify the wired value without investigation.
findings.finding: 'The LLM gates … honor a wired timeout no lower than the SDK default' names a floor constraint but does not identify which SDK(s) are in scope, leaving the executor unable to look up the default value without investigation.
findings.location: Success Criteria bullet: 'The LLM gates fail closed on unreadable config and honor a wired timeout no lower than the SDK default'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a parenthetical: '…no lower than the SDK default (e.g. openai SDK: 600 s; anthropic SDK: 600 s)' or name the single SDK used by this codebase.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace 'docs are navigable by humans' with a concrete, in-session-evaluable proxy (e.g. 'a docs/index.md exists with links to all top-level guide pages; all internal doc links resolve with no 404s as verified by a link-check CI step').
findings.finding: 'docs are navigable by humans' is a subjective, non-measurable criterion with no observable, evaluable proxy.
findings.location: Success Criteria bullet: 'Docs drift called out by the audit is fixed; CodeQL runs on the mirror; docs are navigable by humans; repo hygiene items closed'
findings.suggested_fix: State: 'docs/index.md exists and links to all top-level guides; CI link-checker reports zero broken internal links'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Align the terminology for the git-store write-path remedy: use a single consistent term (e.g. 'push-merge lock') in both the Success Criteria bullet and AC bullet 2, or split into two distinct named items if they are genuinely different mitigations.
findings.finding: The term for the git-store write-path race remedy differs between sections: Success Criteria calls it 'push-retry merge' while AC bullet 2 calls it 'push-merge lock'. These could refer to the same mechanism or two distinct mitigations; as written they are contradictory.
findings.location: Success Criteria bullet 2 vs Acceptance Criteria bullet 2

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add or tighten AC bullet 2 to explicitly require a two-clone integration test for the cross-clone claim race, matching the success criterion's 'pinned by a two-clone test' language.
findings.finding: Success Criteria bullet 1 specifies that the claim-race fix must be 'pinned by a two-clone test' as a concrete verification mechanism, but AC bullet 2 only requires the child story to be 'landed with regression tests' — generic language that does not verify the two-clone scenario exists.
findings.location: Success Criteria bullet 1 vs Acceptance Criteria bullet 2
findings.suggested_fix: Amend AC bullet 2 to: '…claim-race surfacing (verified by a two-clone integration test)…'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document a rollback/migration path for the unmapped-status guard change: specify how existing unmapped-status-reliant workflows can add explicit mappings, and provide a rollback procedure if the guard causes unexpected ticket-state freezes.
findings.finding: The 'unmapped-status guard' child suppresses a previously silent behavior (unmapped Jira statuses silently reopening local tickets), but the plan provides no migration path, rollback, or grace period for integrations that relied on this implicit reopen behavior.
findings.location: Success Criteria: 'Inbound Jira statuses that have no mapping can no longer silently reopen local tickets'; Acceptance Criteria: 'unmapped-status guard'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a doc/ADR callout (contributing guide section or ADR entry) for the env-var registry pattern, specifying its schema, how contributors register a new env var, and what the CI drift gate validates.
findings.finding: The env-var registry + CI drift gate is a new mandatory contract for declaring environment variables, but the plan names no ADR, contributing-guide section, or doc artifact that explains the registry's schema, how to add a new env var, and what the CI gate checks — leaving the new pattern undocumented.
findings.location: Success Criteria: 'env-var registry with a CI drift gate'; Acceptance Criteria: 'Maintainability children (mypy ratchet start, env-var registry + drift gate) landed'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Call out a doc/ADR artifact (or inline API-doc update) for the new claim-race error surface and push-merge lock contract, so callers know what to catch and what the locking semantics are.
findings.finding: The claim-race surfacing child introduces a new error contract (the losing claimant now receives an explicit signal), and the push-merge lock child introduces a new locking contract, but the plan calls out no doc or ADR for either new caller-visible contract.
findings.location: Success Criteria: 'The store's cross-clone claim race is surfaced to the losing claimant'; 'No git-store write path can commit another write's leftovers or race the push-retry merge'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR (or CLAUDE.md / design-doc section) for each novel architectural pattern introduced: the env-var registry + CI drift gate, the push-merge lock concurrency contract, and the mypy strictness ratchet baseline — and include this as a deliverable in the Docs AC row.
findings.finding: The plan introduces several novel architectural patterns (env-var registry + CI drift gate, push-merge lock, mypy strictness ratchet) but does not specify capturing any ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or design document for these new cross-cutting decisions. The 'docs children' listed in the AC address only pre-existing drift (stale shell-test citations, stale architecture remedy row, api-stability version, docs index/user guide), not the new patterns introduced by this plan itself.
findings.location: Success Criteria / Acceptance Criteria — 'Maintainability children' and 'Docs children' rows
findings.suggested_fix: Add one AC bullet: '[ ] ADR or CLAUDE.md section created for: (a) env-var registry contract and CI drift gate; (b) git-store push-merge lock invariants; (c) mypy ratchet baseline and graduation path.' This can be a single lightweight doc (e.g., `docs/adr/0001-reliability-seam-remediation.md`) rather than three separate files.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] For each of the three behavior-changing items (unmapped-status guard, git-store write lock, LLM fail-closed config), add a brief note to the relevant child story acknowledging known callers and confirming the new behavior is safe for them (or listing any required call-site updates).
findings.finding: Three behavior-changing items (unmapped-status guard, git-store write lock, LLM fail-closed default) alter existing runtime behavior, but the plan does not acknowledge any existing callers or downstream consumers that may depend on the old behavior — e.g., a workflow that relied on silent reopen of unmapped statuses, or a caller of the git-store write path that now contends on a new lock.
findings.location: Success Criteria bullets 2, 3, 4 / overall plan body

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a positive rationale to the LLM-timeout-wiring child story explaining that `timeout_s` must be threaded into the pydantic-ai `model_settings` dict (the SDK-level seam that propagates to every provider's httpx client), and confirm it applies to both the NativeOutput and PromptedOutput execution paths in `runner.py`.
findings.finding: The plan's LLM-timeout-wiring child has no positive rationale for HOW the `timeout_s` config value will be wired into the SDK call. Code inspection shows `LLMConfig.timeout_s` (default 600s) is resolved and stored but never passed to `agent.run_sync()` or included in `model_settings` in `runner.py` — so the field is currently a no-op at runtime. The plan's AC says 'honor a wired timeout no lower than the SDK default' but does not specify whether the wiring targets the pydantic-ai `model_settings` dict (per-call, SDK-level), an httpx client-level timeout, or an external threading wrapper. These differ meaningfully in scope and correctness: SDK model_settings is the right seam (it propagates through pydantic-ai's provider adapters to the underlying httpx client), while a threading wrapper would terminate the Python thread but leave the network call orphaned. Without a stated rationale, an implementer may choose the wrong mechanism.
findings.location: Success Criteria / AC: 'LLM gates fail closed on unreadable config and honor a wired timeout no lower than the SDK default'; src/rebar/llm/runner.py PydanticAIRunner.run() model_settings block (~L268-279); src/rebar/llm/config.py LLMConfig.timeout_s field
findings.suggested_fix: In the child story body, add: 'Approach: pass `model_settings["timeout"] = cfg.timeout_s` in `PydanticAIRunner.run()` alongside `max_tokens`, before the `agent.run_sync()` call. This is the pydantic-ai ModelSettings seam that propagates the timeout through each provider's httpx client. Apply to both the NativeOutput path (the `Agent(model, output_type=mode_obj, ...)` constructor) and the PromptedOutput `run_sync` call in `_pai_structured`. Verify with a test that mocks `run_sync` and asserts the timeout appears in model_settings.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the GHAS enablement status of the repo and add `security-events: write` to the planned codeql.yml `permissions` block, or document a fallback (e.g. use CodeQL as a pure SARIF artifact without the upload step if GHAS is unavailable).
findings.finding: The CodeQL child story integrates a new GitHub Advanced Security (GHAS) surface — uploading SARIF to the 'Security → Code scanning' tab — but the plan states neither the required `security-events: write` Actions permission nor whether GHAS is enabled on the repo. Without GHAS enabled (required for private repos), the `github/codeql-action/upload-sarif` step will fail or silently no-op, and `security-events: write` must be declared in the workflow's `permissions` block or uploads are rejected.
findings.location: Success Criteria bullet 5 ('CodeQL runs on the mirror'); OSS/CI children AC bullet 4
findings.suggested_fix: In the CodeQL child story, explicitly assert whether the repo is public (GHAS free) or private (GHAS license required), add `permissions: security-events: write` to the planned workflow, and add a preflight note: 'verify that Settings → Code security → Code scanning is enabled before landing this child.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace 'no lower than the SDK default' with a concrete numeric floor and the specific SDK layer it applies to (e.g. 'no lower than 600s, wired as httpx `read_timeout` or pydantic-ai `timeout` kwarg on `agent.run_sync()`'), and confirm how `cfg.timeout_s` is threaded from `LLMConfig` into the actual SDK call.
findings.finding: The plan's Success Criterion 'honor a wired timeout no lower than the SDK default' references 'SDK default' as a lower-bound value vocabulary but never defines it: which SDK (pydantic-ai? httpx? anthropic-python?), what its default timeout value is, and how the plan's constraint ('no lower than') is operationalized. Tool inspection confirms `timeout_s` (DEFAULT_TIMEOUT_S=600) exists in `LLMConfig.from_env` but is never passed to `agent.run_sync()` in `PydanticAIRunner.run()` — the 'SDK default' the plan intends to floor against is undefined in the plan text.
findings.location: Success Criteria bullet 3: 'honor a wired timeout no lower than the SDK default'
findings.suggested_fix: State explicitly: 'The configured `REBAR_LLM_TIMEOUT` (default 600s) is wired into `agent.run_sync(timeout=cfg.timeout_s)` (or equivalent httpx timeout); the floor ensures the gate cannot time out faster than the configured value regardless of SDK-level defaults.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a fallback specification for the unmapped-status guard: state explicitly whether an unmapped inbound status (a) blocks reconciliation for that ticket and emits a typed alert, (b) applies a safe-default status (and which one), or (c) skips the field-update only and alerts — so the implementing child story has a verifiable, unambiguous output contract.
findings.finding: The plan names 'unmapped-status guard' as a reliability child with regression tests (AC bullet 2) but provides no fallback specification for the sub-step that guards inbound Jira statuses: what happens when the guard fires (raise? log-and-skip? emit an alert? block the reconciler pass entirely?), and what the 'guard' emits to the losing code path. This is a T8(d) gap for an incomplete/failed sub-step fallback.
findings.location: Success Criteria bullet 4 / AC bullet 2: 'unmapped-status guard'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
